title: When did the sleep story start?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for tracing where the "I can't sleep" identity came from — and whether it was ever really yours.
excerpt: A journal prompt set from Day 11 of the SLEEP program. The prompts ask for the origin story: when the "I'm a bad sleeper" label first appeared, who assigned it, and whether you ever had a chance to push back.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 11 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795), "Heal the 'I Can't Sleep' Story."

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to understand where the "bad sleeper" identity came from before trying to change it — particularly useful early in a sleep programme.

content.text: A journal prompt set from Day 11 of the SLEEP program. The prompts ask for the origin story of the sleep identity: when it started, who assigned it, and whether you ever had a chance to question it. Twenty minutes, no editing.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
What is my current sleep story — the version I tell when someone asks how I sleep?
Write it out exactly as you would say it. The words you use. The tone.
How long have I been telling that story?
Years, or decades? Was there a time before it?
When did the story first appear? What was happening in my life at the time?
Try to locate the specific period — an age, a season, a circumstance. What else was difficult then?
Whose voice does the story use? Did someone tell me I was a bad sleeper, or did I conclude it myself?
A parent, a partner, a doctor? Or did a string of hard nights become a permanent identity?
Has the story ever been wrong — a night, a week, a period where I slept better than the story allows?
Write the exception. The holiday, the exhausted night, the pregnancy, whenever sleep came more easily.
Adapted from Day 11 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
